threats.title: Unrestricted Access to mintYieldFee Allows Theft of All Yield Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The `mintYieldFee` function lacks access control and ignores the configured `_yieldFeeRecipient`, allowing any caller to mint the accrued yield fees to themselves.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function is external and has no `onlyOwner` or `msg.sender == _yieldFeeRecipient` checks. It directly mints `_shares` to the user-provided `_recipient`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing access control modifier and ignores _yieldFeeRecipient state variable

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Anyone can call mintYieldFee and specify themselves as the recipient

operational_questions: Can an attacker monitor for yield fee accrual and immediately mint them?
validation_requirements: Verify mintYieldFee can be called by non-admins

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for yield to be liquidated and _yieldFeeTotalSupply to increase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Receive Vault shares representing the yield fees

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of protocol revenue (yield fees)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to mintYieldFee by unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Yield fee recipient receiving no fees

threats.title: Idle Underlying Assets in Vault Subsidize Arbitrary Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_deposit` function only transfers `_assets - _vaultAssets` from the caller. If the vault holds idle underlying assets, a depositor can receive shares without transferring the full asset amount, effectively stealing the idle assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The logic `_assetsDeposit = _assets - _vaultAssets` uses the vault's existing balance to fulfill the user's deposit, granting them shares backed by assets they didn't provide.
preconditions: Vault holds a balance of the underlying asset > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Depositor pays less than the requested deposit amount
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever idle assets exist in the vault

operational_questions: How do idle assets accumulate in the vault?
validation_requirements: Test deposit when vault has pre-existing balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create idle assets in the vault (e.g., via direct transfer or incomplete liquidation sweep)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call deposit() with _assets equal to the idle balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Receive shares without any tokens being transferred from the attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Redeem shares to extract the stolen assets

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of idle assets, diluting existing shareholders

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deposits where transferFrom amount is less than minted shares value
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Deposits immediately following direct asset transfers

threats.title: Arbitrary Delegation Override via Sponsor Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The `sponsor` function allows anyone to deposit on behalf of a receiver and automatically forces the receiver's TWAB balance to be delegated to the SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS, revoking their prize eligibility.
threats.investigation_rationale: `_sponsor` calls `_twabController.sponsor(_receiver)` without checking if the caller has authority over the receiver's delegation preferences.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Calls _twabController.sponsor(_receiver) unconditionally if not already sponsored

preconditions: Victim has a non-zero TWAB balance and is not currently delegating to the sponsorship address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Third party can alter a user's delegation state

operational_questions: Can users prevent this delegation override?
validation_requirements: Test if sponsor(1, victim) successfully revokes victim's prize eligibility

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a user with a large TWAB balance likely to win prizes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's entire balance is delegated to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim wins no prizes in subsequent draws

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted DoS of prize eligibility for specific users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: User must manually re-delegate, but can be attacked again

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sponsor calls for 1 wei targeting large holders
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large holders suddenly delegated to sponsorship address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated sponsor calls targeting different users

threats.title: Unit Mismatch in Liquidate Asset Sweep Leaves Assets Vulnerable
threats.hypothesis_description: In `liquidate`, the condition `_amountOut >= _vaultAssets` compares Vault shares (`_amountOut`) to underlying assets (`_vaultAssets`). If the exchange rate is > 1:1, this check can fail even when the shares represent more value than the assets, leaving assets idle in the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: Comparing shares directly to assets is mathematically unsound when the exchange rate deviates from 1:1, leading to skipped deposits into the yield vault.
preconditions: Vault has idle underlying assets during liquidation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assets are not swept into the yield vault during liquidation

operational_questions: Does this reliably leave assets in the vault?
validation_requirements: Simulate liquidation with exchange rate > 1 and observe vault asset balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for exchange rate to exceed 1:1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Trigger liquidation where _amountOut < _vaultAssets but value is sufficient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Assets remain in the vault instead of being deposited to yield vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit H2 (_deposit subsidization) to steal the idle assets

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Creates idle assets that can be stolen

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Vault holding underlying asset balances after liquidation
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Yield vault deposits skipped during liquidation

threats.title: Canary Prize Claims Force Premature Tier Expansion
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_computeNextNumberOfTiers` logic sets `_nextNumberOfTiers = largestTierClaimed + 2`. If a canary prize is claimed, this unconditionally expands the number of tiers, bypassing the `claimExpansionThreshold` checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The initial assignment of `_nextNumberOfTiers` based on `largestTierClaimed` can exceed `_numTiers` without satisfying the strict claim count thresholds intended to govern expansion.
preconditions: A canary prize (tier = numberOfTiers - 1) is won and claimed

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tier expansion occurs without meeting claim count thresholds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any draw where a canary prize is won

operational_questions: Does a single canary claim always trigger expansion?
validation_requirements: Test tier expansion with 1 canary claim and 0 normal claims

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor draws for a winning canary prize
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The next draw will have numberOfTiers + 1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Prize liquidity is diluted across more tiers than economically justified

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Dilution of prize sizes, reducing user incentives
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low (tiers will contract if not claimed)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Tier expansion with low overall claim counts

threats.title: Malicious afterClaimPrize Hook Can DoS Batch Prize Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: A winner can configure an `afterClaimPrize` hook that intentionally reverts. Because `Vault.claimPrizes` processes claims in batches, this reverting hook will cause the entire batch transaction to fail, griefing the claimer.
threats.investigation_rationale: External calls to user-controlled contracts within a loop without `try/catch` allow a single malicious actor to disrupt the batch processing of claims.
preconditions: Claimer includes the winner in a batch claim

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Batch claims revert due to a single winner's hook

operational_questions: Can claimers easily identify and exclude malicious hooks?
validation_requirements: Test batch claim with one reverting hook

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy a smart contract that reverts on afterClaimPrize
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Set this contract as the hook for a winning address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for a third-party claimer to include the address in a batch

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Increased friction and gas costs for claimers

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Specific winners repeatedly causing claim reverts

threats.title: Accumulated Precision Loss in _consumeLiquidity Depletes Reserve
threats.hypothesis_description: The conversion from `UD60x18` to `UD34x4` in `_consumeLiquidity` truncates 14 decimal places. Over thousands of claims, this precision loss accumulates, causing the tier's remaining liquidity to be artificially inflated and forcing the protocol to over-consume the reserve to cover the difference.
threats.investigation_rationale: Repeated truncation in a state-accumulating variable (`prizeTokenPerShare`) leads to systemic accounting drift over time.
preconditions: High volume of prize claims across many draws

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reserve is consumed faster than expected

operational_questions: How much precision is lost per average draw?
validation_requirements: Simulate 10,000 claims and measure reserve drift

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Each claim loses up to 1e14 wei of precision in tracking
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tier appears to have liquidity when it is actually empty
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subsequent claims pull from the reserve, eventually depleting it

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Premature reserve depletion, potential reverts on legitimate claims

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between accounted liquidity and actual tokens

threats.title: Unrestricted contributePrizeTokens Allows Stealing Contribution Credit
threats.hypothesis_description: `contributePrizeTokens` determines the contribution amount by checking the pool's unaccounted token balance. An attacker can back-run a victim's token transfer and call `contributePrizeTokens` for their own vault, stealing the victim's contribution credit and inflating their vault's prize odds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks access control and relies on the global token balance delta, making it vulnerable to race conditions if token transfer and contribution are not atomic.
preconditions: Victim transfers tokens to PrizePool in a separate transaction from calling contributePrizeTokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contribution credit can be assigned to any vault
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between token transfer and contribute call

operational_questions: Do legitimate workflows ever split transfer and contribute into separate transactions?
validation_requirements: Test back-running a transfer with a different vault address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for large ERC20 transfers to the PrizePool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before the sender can call contributePrizeTokens, call it with attacker's vault address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker's vault receives the contribution credit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker's vault users have artificially inflated odds of winning

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of contribution credit, unfair prize distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: contributePrizeTokens called by an address unrelated to the token sender
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Vaults receiving credit without transferring tokens

threats.title: uint96 Truncation in closeDraw Loses Excess Contributions
threats.hypothesis_description: In `closeDraw`, the total contributions for the draw are cast to `uint96`. If the prize token has high decimals or a large supply (e.g., PEPE, SHIB) and contributions exceed `type(uint96).max` (~79.2 billion with 18 decimals), the value is silently truncated, permanently losing the excess contributions from distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_contributionsForDraw` function returns a `uint256`, but it is explicitly downcast to `uint96` when passed to `_nextDraw`, causing silent overflow.
deployment_assumptions: Prize token is a high-supply or high-decimal token where contributions can exceed 79.2 billion units
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided, but risk depends on the chosen prizeToken
preconditions: Contributions for a single draw exceed type(uint96).max

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Downcasting uint256 to uint96 without safe cast checks

operational_questions: What tokens are intended to be used as prize tokens?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol operates with a high-supply token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Contributions for a draw exceed ~79.2 billion
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: closeDraw is called, truncating the value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Excess tokens remain in the contract but are never distributed as prizes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of prize liquidity from distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High (requires admin intervention to extract unaccounted tokens)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Accounted balance diverging significantly from actual token balance
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Small prize sizes despite large contributions

threats.title: Unnecessary Infinite Approval to Liquidation Pair Risks Asset Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: `Vault.setLiquidationPair` grants the new liquidation pair an infinite approval to spend the vault's underlying asset. The liquidation process does not require the LP to pull underlying assets. If the LP is compromised or malicious, it can drain all underlying assets from the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: Principle of least privilege violation. The LP only needs to call `liquidate` to mint shares; it does not need ERC20 approval for the underlying asset.
deployment_assumptions: Owner sets a malicious or vulnerable LiquidationPair
preconditions: Liquidation pair is malicious or compromised

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Granting max approval to a contract that doesn't need it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While a vulnerable/malicious LP is set

operational_questions: Does the LP ever legitimately need to pull underlying assets?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner sets a vulnerable LP contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker exploits the LP contract to call transferFrom on the vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker drains all underlying assets held by the vault

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of underlying assets in the vault

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected transferFrom calls originating from the LP

threats.title: 100% Yield Fee Causes Permanent DoS of Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: If the Vault owner sets `yieldFeePercentage` to `FEE_PRECISION` (1e9, representing 100%), the `liquidate` function will revert due to a division by zero when calculating the yield fee. This permanently halts the conversion of yield to prizes.
threats.investigation_rationale: `_setYieldFeePercentage` uses `>` instead of `>=` when checking against `FEE_PRECISION`, allowing it to be exactly 100%. The math in `liquidate` subtracts this from `FEE_PRECISION` in the denominator.
deployment_assumptions: Owner mistakenly or maliciously sets yield fee to 1e9

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Setting fee to 100% breaks core functionality

operational_questions: Is there any valid reason to set the fee to 100%?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts with division by zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Yield cannot be converted to prizes, breaking the protocol loop

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of prize funding for the vault
